medications.indication_rationale: May be offered PRN for the mild myalgia/arthralgia as symptomatic relief only. It does not treat the fatigue and is not central to management; use standard over-the-counter labelling — no dose is synthesised here because the source note did not specify one.

There is no evidence of an active bacterial infection — the preceding illness was an uncomplicated viral URTI that has resolved. Prescribing antibiotics for post-viral fatigue is inappropriate and an error of commission.

An antidepressant should NOT be started as a reflex treatment for this fatigue. The picture is post-exertional and post-infectious rather than a primary depressive disorder; starting an SSRI without a proper mood assessment psychologises the presentation and is a common error. (It may be appropriate only if a comorbid depressive disorder is later identified on assessment.)

The patient has already found that large amounts of caffeine gave no energy benefit and provoked palpitations. Recommending stimulants or 'powering through' with caffeine worsens symptoms and is contraindicated in this context.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Occupational therapy can help with energy-conservation strategies, activity pacing and practical adaptations (e.g. a shower chair) to reduce the energetic cost of daily activities in a severely deconditioned patient.
The patient lives alone with severe functional impairment; social work / home supports (temporary meal delivery, welfare checks) address safety and the energetic cost of self-care.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Adopt strict activity pacing: treat available energy like a phone battery stuck at 20% that charges slowly — stop any activity at the FIRST slight increase in symptoms, well before feeling exhausted, and break tasks into small increments (e.g. wash three plates, rest 15 minutes, then continue).
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Pacing keeps the patient within their energy envelope and prevents the push-and-crash cycle that drives post-exertional malaise; it is the central evidence-informed self-management strategy (NICE NG206).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do NOT attempt graded exercise or 'push through' the fatigue to build fitness; avoid all strenuous physical and high-demand cognitive tasks during crashes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Graded exercise therapy and pushing through are harmful in post-exertional malaise and provoke prolonged crashes; avoiding them is protective.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep a regular night-time sleep routine; rest in a dark, quiet room during the day WITHOUT sleeping, to avoid disrupting the nocturnal sleep rhythm.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Supports sleep consolidation and provides sensory rest without daytime sleep that could fragment night sleep.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid relying on caffeine or energy drinks for energy; maintain regular fluids and meals, using temporary meal delivery to reduce the energetic cost of cooking.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Caffeine gave no benefit and provoked palpitations; reducing the energetic cost of self-care supports recovery in an isolated patient.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like fatigue following your recent viral illness, but it is a diagnosis we can only make once some blood tests (and a heart tracing) come back normal — those tests rule out other treatable causes like thyroid problems or low iron.

Recovery is usually slow and up-and-down over weeks to months. The most important thing you can do is pace yourself and avoid pushing through, because overexertion causes the crashes and can set you back.
Caffeine and 'powering through' won't fix this and can make it worse — your body needs structured rest, not stimulation.
Because you live alone and are so limited right now, it's worth arranging some help — meal delivery, someone to check on you, and aids like a shower chair to save energy.

follow_up_plan.interval: GP review in 1-2 weeks to review the blood results and ECG and to set up a longer-term pacing and graded return-to-activity plan
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Blood panel and ECG results; symptom trajectory and functional status; screen for a comorbid mood disorder; home-support needs and safety of living alone
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If chest pain, breathlessness at rest, or a racing/irregular heartbeat develops — call 000. If unable to keep fluids down, rapidly losing weight, developing drenching night sweats, or joints become hot/red/swollen — seek prompt review sooner than the planned appointment.

must_include_items: Recognise the likely post-infectious fatigue picture without prematurely closing the diagnosis
Arrange outpatient bloods (FBC, U&E, LFTs, TFTs, iron studies, CRP) and an ECG to exclude treatable organic and cardiac causes
Advise activity pacing / staying within an energy envelope and explicitly avoid recommending graded or push-through exercise
Provide GP follow-up within 1-2 weeks to review results and establish a longer-term plan
Safety-net for cardiac and constitutional red flags (000/ED for chest pain, breathlessness at rest, racing/irregular heartbeat, or inability to keep fluids down)
Do NOT over-triage the haemodynamically stable patient to emergency care on the basis of the alarming framing
acceptable_alternatives: Arranging the workup and follow-up via the GP by telehealth rather than in person is acceptable
Referral to a fatigue/CFS service or general physician for the exclusion workup is an acceptable route to the same organic screen
PRN simple analgesia for myalgia is an acceptable optional adjunct
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any organic workup (bloods +/- ECG) before attributing symptoms to a post-viral cause
Failing to safety-net the cardiac red flags
Not addressing that the patient lives alone with severe functional impairment
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging to ED or calling 000 for a stable presentation based on the alarming framing
Recommending graded exercise therapy or 'pushing through' the fatigue
Starting an antidepressant reflexively without a mood assessment
Recommending high-dose caffeine or stimulants for energy
Declaring myocarditis 'excluded' on the basis of the chat alone
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A passing plan must (1) arrange an organic exclusion workup (bloods, and ideally an ECG) rather than either dismissing it as needing no review OR escalating to emergency care, (2) give correct activity-pacing advice while avoiding graded/push-through exercise, and (3) safety-net the cardiac red flags. Over-triage to ED/000 and recommending graded exercise are both significant errors even if other content is good.
